items.title: Publish the client as a .NET 10 Windows EXE
items.description: Build the distributable client with dotnet publish targeting win-x64. The output is a runnable EXE plus its files. Choose framework-dependent or self-contained deployment depending on whether the target machine has the matching .NET runtime.
items.applicability: Build and packaging step that produces the client a non-developer will run.
items.example: dotnet publish -c Release -r win-x64 produces ScreenStreamClient.exe in the publish folder ready to copy to the target PC.
items.limitation: A publish on one machine targets a specific runtime identifier and architecture; an x64 build will not run on an ARM64 device, and an OS far older than the .NET 10 support matrix is unsupported.

items.title: Choose framework-dependent vs self-contained deliberately
items.description: Framework-dependent builds are small but require the .NET 10 runtime preinstalled. Self-contained builds bundle the runtime, run on a clean machine, but are larger. For non-developers on unknown machines, prefer self-contained to avoid a 'install .NET first' step.
items.example: dotnet publish -r win-x64 --self-contained true yields a larger folder that runs on a fresh Windows 11 Home PC without installing any runtime.
items.limitation: Self-contained increases download size to tens of megabytes and ships a private runtime copy the operator must keep patched; framework-dependent shifts patching to the shared system runtime instead.

items.title: Server generates a ready-to-run client package
items.description: The server side provides a client-EXE generator: given a session, it produces a folder containing the client EXE, a client-config.json (server address, port, options), the session token, a checksum file, and a README. The operator hands this folder to the person who will share their screen.
items.applicability: Operator workflow for distributing a configured client.
items.example: Clicking 'Generate client' writes ./client-7QF3/ with ScreenStreamClient.exe, client-config.json, token.txt, checksums.txt, and README.txt prefilled for session 7QF3.
items.limitation: The generator can only embed configuration it knows at generation time; if the server's public address changes later, a previously generated package points at a stale address and must be regenerated.

items.title: Write client-config.json with server address, port, and token
items.description: The generated client reads its target from a config file rather than requiring the user to type anything. client-config.json holds the server address, port, session token, and display options. The user just runs the EXE; the config supplies the rest.
items.applicability: Configuration contract between generator and client.
items.example: client-config.json: { "server": "203.0.113.10", "port": 8080, "useTls": true, "token": "...", "fps": 10 } sits next to the EXE and is loaded on launch.
items.limitation: A plaintext token in a config file is only as protected as the folder it sits in; the operator must transfer the package over a trusted channel and treat the token as a short-lived secret.

items.title: Include a checksum file for the generated package
items.description: Write a checksums file (e.g. SHA-256 of the EXE and config) into the package so the recipient or operator can verify the files were not altered in transit. The README explains how to check it.
items.applicability: Integrity verification of a distributed client package.
items.example: checksums.txt lists 'ScreenStreamClient.exe  <sha256>'; the operator can run Get-FileHash to confirm the EXE matches before the user runs it.
items.limitation: A checksum proves integrity only if obtained over a trusted channel; an attacker who can alter the EXE can also alter the checksum file, so it guards against accidental corruption more than a determined tamperer.

items.title: Use simple folder and EXE names for non-developers
items.description: Name the package folder and executable in plain, friendly terms a non-technical person recognises, avoiding cryptic build hashes or version soup. The person should see an obvious thing to double-click.
items.example: The folder is 'ScreenShare for Session 7QF3' and the file is 'Start Screen Sharing.exe' rather than 'ssc-0.9.3-win-x64-rel.exe'.
items.limitation: Friendly names can collide if two packages land in the same directory; include a short session id in the folder name to keep them distinct without becoming cryptic.

items.title: Provide a Start-Server.bat for the operator
items.description: Ship a small Start-Server.bat (or .ps1) that launches the server with the right working directory and arguments, so the operator starts everything with a double-click instead of remembering a command line.
items.example: Start-Server.bat contains 'cd /d %~dp0' then 'ScreenStreamServer.exe --config server-config.json' so a double-click starts the server from its own folder.
items.limitation: A batch file runs whatever it contains with the user's privileges; operators should read it before running, and it cannot fix a genuinely misconfigured server, only launch it consistently.

items.title: Expect a Windows Firewall prompt on first run
items.description: The first time the server listens on a port, Windows Defender Firewall typically prompts the user to Allow access. The operator must click Allow (at least for the relevant network profile) or no client can connect. Document this as an expected, required step.
items.example: On first launch a 'Windows Defender Firewall has blocked some features' dialog appears; the README instructs the operator to tick the network type and click Allow access.
items.limitation: If the user clicks Cancel/Block, or lacks rights to change firewall rules, connections silently fail; on managed/corporate machines a policy may block the prompt entirely and require an administrator.

items.title: Two home PCs cannot connect over the internet without a public address
items.description: On typical Windows 11 Home setups behind consumer routers, both machines have private addresses behind NAT. Two such PCs in different homes cannot connect directly over the internet unless one side has a reachable public address (public IP + port-forward) or a relay/tunnel is used. This is a NAT reality, not a bug.
items.applicability: Setting honest expectations before attempting an internet connection.
items.example: Alice at home and Bob at home both sit behind NAT; Bob's client cannot reach Alice's server at its 192.168.x.x address from across the internet, so a direct connection fails by design.
items.limitation: Some routers offer NAT-PMP/UPnP auto port-mapping or carrier-grade NAT prevents port-forwarding entirely; capabilities vary by ISP and router, so the only safe assumption is 'not reachable until proven otherwise'.

items.title: LAN mode: same network plus firewall allowed
items.description: When both machines are on the same local network (same Wi-Fi/router), the client can reach the server by its LAN address once the firewall allows the port. This is the simplest reliable mode and should be the default for demos and same-site use.
items.example: Server listens on 0.0.0.0:8080; the client config points at the server's LAN address 192.168.1.50:8080; with the firewall allowed, frames flow on the local network.
items.limitation: LAN mode only works when both machines are genuinely on the same network; guest-network isolation or client-isolation Wi-Fi settings can block peer-to-peer traffic even on the same SSID.

items.title: Fixed public IP with router port-forwarding for internet use
items.description: To reach a server across the internet, the server's router must forward the chosen external port to the server machine's internal address, and the operator connects to the router's public IP. A static/known public IP (or dynamic DNS) makes the address stable.
items.applicability: Operator-led internet exposure of the server.
items.example: The router forwards external TCP 8080 to 192.168.1.50:8080; remote clients connect to the public IP (or a DDNS hostname) on 8080.
items.limitation: Port-forwarding exposes a service to the whole internet and requires router-admin access; many ISPs use carrier-grade NAT where no inbound forwarding is possible, and a dynamic public IP changes without DDNS.

items.title: A tunnel/relay is an optional later operator step
items.description: When port-forwarding is impossible or undesirable, an operator may later put a tunnel/relay in front (a service that gives a public endpoint and forwards to the local server). This is an explicit, operator-chosen step layered on top, not a built-in product capability, and must use a trusted relay.
items.applicability: Advanced connectivity options documented for operators.
items.example: An operator runs a tunnel that publishes a public HTTPS URL forwarding to localhost:8080, then shares that URL's address with the remote client.
items.limitation: Relays route traffic through a third party, adding latency and a trust dependency; using one is the operator's deliberate decision with its own security and privacy implications, and is out of scope for the core tool.

items.title: TLS/WSS is required for internet production
items.description: Any session that crosses the public internet must use TLS (WSS, not plain WS). Without it, frames and the token traverse the network in the clear. A valid certificate for the server's public name is required for production internet use.
items.applicability: Transport security policy for non-LAN deployments.
items.example: Production config sets useTls=true and the server presents a certificate for its public hostname so the client connects over wss:// with encryption.
items.limitation: TLS protects data in transit but not a compromised endpoint; self-signed certificates trigger trust warnings, and obtaining a valid certificate requires a resolvable public hostname.

items.title: Do not fake internet reachability
items.description: Never claim or imply that two arbitrary home machines will connect over the internet 'just by running the EXE'. The product must state reachability honestly: LAN works out of the box; internet needs a public address (port-forward) or an operator-chosen relay, plus TLS. Honest limits prevent broken demos and false promises.
items.applicability: Documentation, UI copy, and acceptance criteria for connectivity claims.
items.example: The README states plainly: 'Same network: works after allowing the firewall. Over the internet: the server must be reachable via a public IP/port-forward or a relay, and must use TLS.'
items.limitation: Honest documentation cannot make an unreachable network reachable; it only sets correct expectations, and operators must still perform the real network configuration.

items.title: Bind the server to an explicit, configurable port and interface
items.description: The server must bind to a configurable port and interface rather than a hidden hard-coded value, so operators can choose a port the firewall/router allows and avoid clashes with other software. Default to a documented port and let it be overridden in config.
items.example: server-config.json sets { "listen": "0.0.0.0", "port": 8080 }; the operator changes the port if 8080 is in use or blocked.
items.limitation: Binding to 0.0.0.0 exposes the port on all interfaces; on an untrusted network the operator may prefer binding to a specific interface, and the chosen port must match the firewall/forwarding rules.

items.title: Write a README the recipient can follow
items.description: Each generated package includes a short README in plain language: what the tool does, that it shares their primary screen with consent, how to start it (double-click), what the firewall prompt means and to click Allow, how to stop (Disconnect), and who to contact. No jargon.
items.applicability: Recipient-facing documentation bundled with every client package.
items.example: README.txt: '1) Double-click Start Screen Sharing. 2) If Windows asks about the firewall, click Allow. 3) Your primary screen is now shared. 4) Click Disconnect to stop.'
items.limitation: A README helps only if read; it cannot substitute for a visible client and clear in-app messages, and it must be kept in sync with the actual UI wording.
